region_of_origin: Korea (popularised in Singapore as a fusion dish)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Buldak Hot Chicken Jjajangmyun weighs approximately 550 g, as recorded from GrabFood menu listings for this dish in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry (SR Legacy) and Korean-style mixed dish component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Buldak Hot Chicken Jjajangmyun. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for wheat noodles (FDC ID 20109), cooked chicken (FDC ID 05064), black bean paste analogue, and vegetable components, weighted by estimated recipe proportions. Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these USDA component estimates and retained where consistent. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted for comparable Korean noodle dish benchmarks.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008); wheat-based Asian noodle analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Buldak Hot Chicken Jjajangmyun. GI estimated at 55 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based Asian noodles (boiled), which range GI 40–60 depending on cooking time and sauce composition. GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. The black bean paste and chilli sauce components add fat and protein that moderately suppress glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 55 × carbohydrate_g 19.09 × serving_g 550) / 10000 = 57.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 2,200 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, reduce black bean paste (chunjang) and soy sauce quantities, increase potassium-rich vegetables such as cabbage and zucchini, and avoid adding extra seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles with estimated GI 55 and total carbohydrate 19.09 g per 100 g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 105 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 58, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to no more than 200 g, substitute wheat noodles with shirataki or lower-GI alternatives, increase vegetable content to add fibre and slow glucose absorption, and reduce added sugar in the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g and saturated fat 1.82 g per 100 g; trans fat 0.04 g per 100 g is low but present; the high-sodium profile is the dominant cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high sodium content; choose a reduced-sodium preparation using low-sodium soy sauce and reduced black bean paste, and ensure the dish is part of a meal with abundant vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g promotes fluid retention and tissue swelling; refined wheat noodles drive pro-inflammatory glycaemic load; nightshade ingredients (gochujang, chilli, potato) may aggravate solanine-sensitive RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should reduce or avoid this dish; if consumed, substitute wheat noodles with a lower-GI alternative, replace potato with sweet potato, retain anti-inflammatory ginger and garlic, and increase cabbage and zucchini portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buldak Hot Chicken Jjajangmyun is a Korean-origin fusion noodle dish that combines two iconic Korean flavour profiles: the fiery, gochujang-and-gochugaru-based heat of Buldak (fire chicken) with the savoury-sweet umami depth of Jjajangmyun (black bean sauce noodles). The dish is built on a base of boiled wheat noodles coated in a stir-fried sauce made from chunjang (fermented black bean paste), gochujang, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and a small amount of sugar, with diced chicken, cabbage, zucchini, onion, and potato stir-fried together. The result is a thick, glossy, dark sauce that is simultaneously spicy, savoury, and mildly sweet, clinging to chewy wheat noodles. The texture is soft-to-chewy from the noodles with tender chicken pieces and slightly softened vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 155 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 6.4 g fat (1.8 g saturated), 19.1 g carbohydrate, 4.6 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g; approximately 2,200 mg per 550 g serving) makes this dish unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients without significant modification. The refined wheat noodle base and moderate-to-high glycaemic load make it a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is popular in Singapore as a Korean fusion restaurant and delivery item, often served as a single-bowl meal. It is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not certified halal due to the chicken and sauce components. Allergens include wheat (gluten), soy, and sesame.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Buldak Hot Chicken Jjajangmyun as a named dish. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central component estimates for wheat noodles, cooked chicken, black bean paste analogue, and vegetables, cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-based Asian noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Korean fusion noodle dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as a named composite dish as of 2026-04-16; values would require laboratory analysis of a standardised recipe. Trans fat 0.04 g per 100 g is retained from the input record; this low level is plausible for a stir-fried dish using small amounts of oil. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was noted but the input record's nutrient provenance is listed as ai_generated with confidence 0.7; accordingly, USDA FoodData Central component-based adaptation is used as the approved source basis. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 55 × carbohydrate_g 19.09 × serving_g 550) / 10000 = 57.7. GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI Database classification; gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. This dish is a fusion variant sold in Singapore; country_of_origin is Singapore for the specific commercial preparation, with Korean culinary origin. FoodEx2 match_type set to 'closest_parent' as the allowed enumerated value closest in meaning to an analogous category-level match.
